1: מוּסָר (mûsār) is the dominant wisdom term for disciplinary instruction — not merely the transfer of information but formative correction that shapes character through consequence. The ben ḥākām (wise son) heeds it; the lēṣ (mocker/scorner) does not even respond to gəʿārâ (rebuke, a sharp verbal correction). The antithesis is absolute: wisdom begins with teachability.
2: נֶפֶשׁ (nephesh) appears twice — the appetite-soul of a person satisfied by the fruit of words (13:2a) and the appetite-soul of the treacherous that craves ḥāmās (violence). The word covers both the deepest self and its hungers; what the inner person feeds on shapes what it becomes. The MKT renders both occurrences as "soul," preserving this continuity.
3: שֹׁמֵר פִּיו (guard/watch his mouth) uses the watchman root šāmar — active vigilance, not passive restraint. The contrast is pōśēq śəpātāyw (one who opens wide his lips): the participle pōśēq implies a permanent disposition, a mouth habitually open. The consequence — mehittâ (ruin/terror) — intensifies this: careless speech is not just socially costly but destructive.
4: נֶפֶשׁ עָצֵל (soul of the lazy) אִוָּתָה (craves, Hithpael of ʾāwâ) — the reflexive stem intensifies the desire: the lazy person craves intensely, but wəʾayin (and nothing). The nephesh ḥārûṣîm (soul of the diligent) is richly satisfied (dušenâ, fat/prosperous). The diligent and the lazy both have deep desire; only one of them acts on it.
5: שֶׁקֶר (šeqer, falsehood/lie) is what the righteous person śānēʾ (hates) — hate is the correct moral response to deception. The wicked person yaḇîš (causes shame/stench) and yaḥpîr (brings disgrace) — both verbs have roots in shame and putrefaction; the MKT "acts disgracefully" captures the sense.
6: צְדָקָה (ṣedāqâ) and רִשְׁעָה (rišʿâ) stand as polar abstractions — righteousness and wickedness as active forces that either guard or overthrow. Both are personified agents in the verse, not merely descriptions. The tammîm dārēk (one of blameless way) is protected by righteousness as though by a sentinel.
7: The verse is deliberately compressed: one person mitrōšēš (Hithpolel of rāšaš, acts rich) yet has nothing; another mitrôšēš (Hithpael of rûš, acts poor) yet has great wealth. The Hithpael stems signal self-presentation — the verse is about performance and appearance versus reality. Both verbs are rare; the contrast is ironic and the form deliberate.
8: כֹּפֶר (kōpher) is the ransom or redemption price — the same word used for the atonement payment in Exodus 30:12 and for the price that averts a penalty. Here it is the rich person's option: wealth can buy off threats. The poor person, paradoxically, does not hear gəʿārâ (rebuke/threatening) at all — they have nothing to be taken, so they are not threatened. Wealth creates vulnerability as well as security.
9: אוֹר צַדִּיקִים (light of the righteous) יִשְׂמָח (rejoices/shines brightly) — the verb is the same as "rejoice"; the MKT "shines with joy" bridges the metaphor. The lamp of the wicked is yidʿak (snuffed out, extinguished) — a sudden, complete ending, not gradual dimming.
10: זָדוֹן (zādôn, presumptuous pride/arrogance) produces only maṣṣâ (contention/strife). The word translated "pride" carries the sense of deliberate, willful presumption — acting as though one does not need God or others. Wisdom (ḥokmâ) is found with those who niwwāʿăṣîm (take counsel together).
11: הוֹן מֵהֶבֶל (wealth from vanity/breath/nothing) — hebel is the same word Ecclesiastes uses for transience and futility; wealth gained that way will similarly vanish (yimʿāṭ, diminish). By contrast, qōbēṣ עַל-יָד (one who gathers hand-by-hand, gradually) will yarbeh (multiply/increase). The contrast is not merely honest vs. dishonest but patient accumulation vs. windfall seeking.
12: תּוֹחֶלֶת מְמֻשָּׁכָה (hope drawn out/prolonged) makes the heart ḥolâ (sick) — the verb root is illness, genuine malaise. The contrast is עֵץ חַיִּים (ʿēṣ ḥayyîm, tree of life) — the Genesis phrase (Proverbs 2:9; Proverbs 3:22, 24) for the source of immortal life. A desire fulfilled carries the weight of restored access to life itself; the delay of hope inverts that promise.
13: בָּז לְדָבָר (despises the word/matter) — dābār can be word, commandment, or matter; the deliberate ambiguity covers both the spoken word and the commandment. יֵחָבֶל לוֹ (will be destroyed for it) — the Niphal passive: destruction comes to the person. יְרֵא מִצְוָה (fears/reverences the commandment) will be yəšullam (rewarded/made complete/paid in full).
14: תּוֹרַת חָכָם (tôrat ḥākām, teaching/torah of the wise) — the word tôrâ carries its full weight: not a suggestion but an authoritative instruction. It is məqôr ḥayyîm (fountain of life), the same phrase used in Psalms 36:9 and Proverbs 14:27 and 16:22 for God himself as the source of life. The sage's instruction participates in that divine vitality.
15: שֵׂכֶל טוֹב (good understanding/insight) wins ḥēn (favor/grace/charm) — the same word used for unmerited divine favor in Genesis 6:8 and Exodus 33:12. The way of the treacherous (בּוֹגְדִים) is ʾêtān (enduring/constant) — the MKT "leads to their ruin" is an interpretation; the Hebrew says their way is "permanent/hard" (suggesting inescapability).
16: כָּל-עָרוּם (every prudent/crafty person) acts from dāʿat (knowledge) — the same root as the knowledge of good and evil; here used positively for deliberate, informed action. The fool "broadcasts his foolishness" — the verb yiphrōś (spreads out, makes known) implies involuntary display: the fool cannot help revealing what he is.
17: מַלְאָךְ רָשָׁע (messenger of wickedness) — the same word malʾāk used for divine angels; the context determines whether the messenger is holy or corrupt. A faithful envoy (ṣîr ʾĕmûnîm, ambassador of faithfulness) brings marpēʾ (healing/health) — the word covers both physical and relational restoration.
18: רֵישׁ וְקָלוֹן (poverty and shame/dishonor) — both are social states, not merely inner ones. The shame (qālôn) that comes with refusing mûsār is public degradation. By contrast, kābôd (honor/weight/glory) comes to the one who accepts tôkaḥat (correction/reproof) — the same word used for God's reproof in the Psalms.
19: תַּאֲוָה נִהְיָה (longing become/fulfilled) is sweet (ʿārēb) to the nephesh — something satisfied at the depth of the self. The second half: it is tôʿăbat kəsîlîm (abomination/detestable) to fools to turn from evil (sûr mērāʿ). The word tôʿăbâ (abomination) is usually what God finds detestable; here fools find the good itself detestable — the inversion is striking.
20: הוֹלֵךְ אֶת-חֲכָמִים יֶחְכָּם (walking with the wise, one becomes wise) — the Qal of ḥākam is not instantaneous; the participle walking suggests an ongoing process of formation. Companionship with fools (rōʿeh kəsîlîm) will be "shattered/broken" — the verb yērôaʿ does not mean merely "suffer harm" but implies the kind of rupture that cannot easily be repaired.
21: חַטָּאִים (sinners) are pursued (təraddēp) by calamity — the verb is the same used for military pursuit; sin is stalked by its consequences. The righteous will be yəšallēm-ṭôb (repaid with good) — šālem (wholeness/completion) as the root: the repayment brings them to completeness.
22: חֵיל חוֹטֵא (wealth of the sinner) is ṣāpûn laṣṣaddîq (stored up for the righteous) — the passive participle ṣāpûn suggests it has been laid away in secret, reserved. Providence steers the flow of accumulated wealth across generations. The word ṣaddîq occurs in both halves — the good man leaves an inheritance, and the righteous ultimately receive the sinner's hoarded wealth.
23: נִיר רָאשִׁים (fallow ground / tillage of the poor/heads) is uncertain — the word rāʾšîm can mean either "poor people" (from rûš) or "heads/chiefs." The MKT follows "poor" as more contextually consistent: their land can yield abundant food (ʾōkel) but it is swept away by lōʾ mišpāṭ (injustice, without justice) — the system, not the soil, is the problem.
24: שֵׁבֶט (šēbeṭ) is the rod/staff — the same word for a tribe's scepter and a shepherd's staff. Discipline and authority converge in the image. חוֹשֵׂךְ שִׁבְטוֹ (the one who withholds his rod) שׂוֹנֵא בְנוֹ (hates his son) — the equation is deliberately shocking. Love (ʾōhăbô, Qal participle, continuous love) disciplines bəšaḥar (diligently/from early on, while there is time).
25: נֶפֶשׁ צַדִּיק (soul of the righteous) תֹּאכַל לְשָׂבַע (eats to satisfaction) — the imperfect verb is customary: this is habitually true, not just once. The belly (beṭen) of the wicked lacks (teḥsar, is deficient) — the same word for the poverty of hunger. Material prosperity follows the contours of character in the wisdom worldview; the connection is structural, not superstitious.
